explicit_text: I was born in Hiroshima in February 1887, and my wife Hideyo was born in Miho, Hiroshima in 1891. Although we left Japan early in the 20th century, our roots remained in Hiroshima. It was a place of origin for us before we emigrated to Hawaii and then the United States. Our connection to Hiroshima shaped our early lives and family ties.
implicit_text: I was born in a city in Japan's Chugoku region in February 1887, and my wife Hideyo was born in a nearby village in 1891. Although we left Japan early in the 20th century, our roots remained in that area. It was a place of origin for us before we emigrated to Hawaii and then the United States. Our connection to that city shaped our early lives and family ties.

explicit_text: My name is Toyosuke Okumura, born in Hiroshima in 1887. I came to Hawaii in 1912 and stayed there for 12 years before moving to the mainland United States in 1924. I was involved in various businesses including grocery, restaurant, and grape growing in California. During World War II, I managed a boarding house in Chicago and later returned to California to continue working until I was about 70 years old.
implicit_text: My name is Toyosuke, born in a city in western Japan in 1887. I came to Hawaii in 1912 and stayed there for 12 years before moving to the mainland United States in 1924. I was involved in various businesses including grocery, restaurant, and grape growing in California. During World War II, I managed a boarding house in Chicago and later returned to California to continue working until I was about 70 years old.

explicit_text: My name is Hideyo Okumura, born in Miho, Hiroshima in 1891. I joined my husband in the United States about four years after he came here, bringing our child with me. I worked as a Japanese language teacher in Hawaii before moving to California where I helped with cooking and managing household duties. During the war, I became ill from overwork but recovered after we returned from Chicago.
implicit_text: My name is Hideyo, born in a village near a major western Japanese city in 1891. I joined my husband in the United States about four years after he came here, bringing our child with me. I worked as a Japanese language teacher in Hawaii before moving to California where I helped with cooking and managing household duties. During the war, I became ill from overwork but recovered after we returned from Chicago.

explicit_text: I first came to Hawaii in 1912 and stayed there for 12 years before moving to the mainland. In Hawaii, I managed a liquor store and later taught at a Japanese language school. Life in Hawaii was not very different from California since many people from my native village lived there. Wages were low in Hawaii compared to the mainland, but I was able to manage and save money.
implicit_text: I first came to the islands in 1912 and stayed there for 12 years before moving to the mainland. In that place, I managed a liquor store and later taught at a Japanese language school. Life there was not very different from California since many people from my native village lived there. Wages were low compared to the mainland, but I was able to manage and save money.

explicit_text: My family and I lived in Sacramento for over 30 years after moving from Hawaii. I worked packing fruits there and also ran a restaurant business for several years. Sacramento was our home base before and after the war, and it was where I was involved in various agricultural contracts, including grape growing in nearby areas.
implicit_text: My family and I lived in a northern California city for over 30 years after moving from the islands. I worked packing fruits there and also ran a restaurant business for several years. That city was our home base before and after the war, and it was where I was involved in various agricultural contracts, including grape growing in nearby areas.

explicit_text: I had a camp in Lodi where I was a contractor growing grapes and hired 30 to 40 Japanese workers, mostly Issei. I cooked meals and prepared Japanese-style baths for the workers, which was hard work. We stored our furniture there when we evacuated during the war, and the government later shipped it to Chicago while we were interned.
implicit_text: I had a camp in a central California town where I was a contractor growing grapes and hired 30 to 40 Japanese workers, mostly first-generation immigrants. I cooked meals and prepared Japanese-style baths for the workers, which was hard work. We stored our furniture there when we evacuated during the war, and the government later shipped it to Chicago while we were interned.

explicit_text: Many of the workers I hired in Lodi were Issei, some married and some single. The Issei faced many difficulties earning a livelihood, especially during the Great Depression, as jobs were scarce. Despite hardships, I believe many Issei persevered and contributed significantly to the Japanese American community.
implicit_text: Many of the workers I hired in that California town were first-generation Japanese immigrants, some married and some single. They faced many difficulties earning a livelihood, especially during the Great Depression, as jobs were scarce. Despite hardships, I believe many persevered and contributed significantly to the Japanese American community.

explicit_text: My religion is Buddhism, specifically the Shinshu sect. It has been an important part of my life and identity since I was in Japan. Even after moving to the United States and facing many challenges, I maintained my faith and practices.
implicit_text: My religion is Buddhism, specifically the Shinshu sect. It has been an important part of my life and identity since I was in Japan. Even after moving to the United States and facing many challenges, I maintained my faith and practices.

explicit_text: I belong to the Shin Buddhism or Shinshu sect, which is a branch of Buddhism. This faith guided me throughout my life, from Japan to Hawaii and then the mainland United States. It provided spiritual support during difficult times such as the war and relocation.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in 1924 after 12 years in Hawaii, seeking better business opportunities. I lived and worked in California for many years, including Sacramento and Lodi, before and after World War II. Despite the challenges of exclusion laws and wartime incarceration, I made a life here with my family and contributed to the community.
implicit_text: I came to the mainland in 1924 after 12 years in the islands, seeking better business opportunities. I lived and worked in California for many years, including in a northern city and a central town, before and after World War II. Despite the challenges of exclusion laws and wartime incarceration, I made a life here with my family and contributed to the community.

explicit_text: When Japan attacked Pearl Harbor in December 1941, we were scared and uncertain about our future. I was living and working in Lodi at the time, and soon after the attack, we faced the freezing of our bank accounts and eventual evacuation. The attack marked the beginning of a very difficult period for Japanese Americans like us.
implicit_text: When the Japanese military attacked the naval base in Hawaii in December 1941, we were scared and uncertain about our future. I was living and working in a central California town at the time, and soon after the attack, we faced the freezing of our bank accounts and eventual evacuation. The attack marked the beginning of a very difficult period for Japanese Americans like us.

explicit_text: Our oldest son graduated from Berkeley and then moved to Chicago to attend university and manage a boarding house. Berkeley was important as the place where he completed his education before the war disrupted our lives. It was a stepping stone for his further studies and work in the United States.
implicit_text: Our oldest son graduated from a university in the San Francisco Bay Area and then moved to Chicago to attend university and manage a boarding house. That city was important as the place where he completed his education before the war disrupted our lives. It was a stepping stone for his further studies and work in the United States.

explicit_text: During the war, after leaving the camp, we went to Chicago where our oldest son managed a boarding house. We stayed there for about three years, and I cooked for many young men who boarded with us. The people in Chicago were kind, and it was a place where we could support each other during difficult times.
implicit_text: During the war, after leaving the camp, we went to a major Midwestern city where our oldest son managed a boarding house. We stayed there for about three years, and I cooked for many young men who boarded with us. The people there were kind, and it was a place where we could support each other during difficult times.

explicit_text: After the Pearl Harbor attack, we were sent to Walerga before being moved to a relocation camp. It was one of the first places we stayed during the forced evacuation. Though it was a difficult time, Walerga was part of the journey many Japanese Americans endured during the war.
implicit_text: After the attack on the naval base, we were sent to a temporary detention center in California before being moved to a relocation camp. It was one of the first places we stayed during the forced evacuation. Though it was a difficult time, that center was part of the journey many Japanese Americans endured during the war.

explicit_text: Some people who wanted to return to Japan were sent to Tule Lake War Relocation Center. We did not go there ourselves, but I know many young people ended up in that camp. Tule Lake was known as a place for those who resisted or were considered disloyal during the war.
implicit_text: Some people who wanted to return to Japan were sent to a northern California relocation center. We did not go there ourselves, but I know many young people ended up in that camp. It was known as a place for those who resisted or were considered disloyal during the war.

explicit_text: The War Relocation Authority was responsible for overseeing the camps where Japanese Americans were held during the war. They sent people to find housing and helped manage the relocation process, including in Chicago where they assisted us. Their role was significant in shaping our wartime experience.
implicit_text: The government agency responsible for overseeing the camps where Japanese Americans were held during the war managed the relocation process. They sent people to find housing and helped manage the relocation process, including in Chicago where they assisted us. Their role was significant in shaping our wartime experience.

explicit_text: The FBI came to investigate us daily, even at night, especially during the war. They questioned me because I taught school, and they also investigated my son George due to his exemption from the Japanese Army draft. Although their presence was stressful, I was never accused of wrongdoing and was eventually left alone.
implicit_text: The federal investigative agency came to question us daily, even at night, especially during the war. They questioned me because I taught school, and they also investigated my son George due to his exemption from the Japanese Army draft. Although their presence was stressful, I was never accused of wrongdoing and was eventually left alone.

explicit_text: We were sent to Camp Amache during the war but stayed there for only about three months before leaving. The camp was one of the places where Japanese Americans were incarcerated, but our stay was relatively short. Afterward, we moved on to Chicago to live with our son.
implicit_text: We were sent to a relocation camp in Colorado during the war but stayed there for only about three months before leaving. The camp was one of the places where Japanese Americans were incarcerated, but our stay was relatively short. Afterward, we moved on to Chicago to live with our son.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, many Issei had difficulty finding jobs and earning a living. We had a camp in Lodi and tried to help those who could not pay their utility bills by lending money. Despite the economic hardships, we managed to find work and support our community.
implicit_text: During the economic downturn of the 1930s, many first-generation Japanese immigrants had difficulty finding jobs and earning a living. We had a camp in central California and tried to help those who could not pay their utility bills by lending money. Despite the economic hardships, we managed to find work and support our community.

explicit_text: The Japanese American Association kept records that the FBI accessed, including information about my son George's draft exemption. It was an organization that connected many in our community and kept track of important matters during the war. Their records sometimes brought unwanted attention from authorities.

It was an organization that connected many in our community and kept track of important matters during the war. Their records sometimes brought unwanted attention from authorities.

explicit_text: The Nisei, or second-generation Japanese Americans, often faced challenges during the war, including questions of loyalty. Some opposed the idea of Nisei going into military service, but my younger son did serve. I believe the Nisei have done well despite the difficulties they encountered.
implicit_text: The second-generation Japanese Americans often faced challenges during the war, including questions of loyalty. Some opposed the idea of second-generation Japanese Americans going into military service, but my younger son did serve. I believe they have done well despite the difficulties they encountered.

explicit_text: The Sansei, or third-generation Japanese Americans, seem to be doing better than the Nisei in many ways. I want to teach them to be frugal and not extravagant, values that helped our family survive hard times. Watching the Sansei grow and succeed gives me hope for the future.
implicit_text: The third-generation Japanese Americans seem to be doing better than the second generation in many ways. I want to teach them to be frugal and not extravagant, values that helped our family survive hard times. Watching the third generation grow and succeed gives me hope for the future.

explicit_text: The Yonsei, or fourth-generation Japanese Americans, are part of our growing family with many grandchildren and great-grandchildren. I hope they will learn from our experiences and continue to live with the values of hard work and frugality. Passing down these lessons is important to me as our family moves forward.
